era_context: The Roman Empire in the early stages of the Third Century Crisis. Emperor Trebonianus Gallus ruled (251–253 CE), facing plague, barbarian invasions, and civil instability. The Sassanid Empire under Shapur I was consolidating power. In China, the Wei-Jin period continued with competing kingdoms. Germanic tribes pressed Roman borders.

events.title: Plague of Cyprian Continues; Trebonianus Gallus Responds
events.description: The Plague of Cyprian, which began around 249–250 CE, continued to ravage the Roman Empire in 252 CE. Emperor Trebonianus Gallus (251–253 CE) attempted to respond to the plague's effects through religious measures, including sacrifices to the gods. The plague caused massive mortality, economic disruption, and social breakdown. Cyprian of Carthage documented the plague's effects in his writings.

sources.name: Cyprian of Carthage, De Mortalitate and correspondence

events.certainty_note: The plague's continuation is documented, but precise annual mortality and Trebonianus Gallus' specific responses are difficult to verify.
cross_references: Part of the Plague of Cyprian (249–262 CE)

events.title: Gothic Invasions in the Balkans and Danube Frontier
events.description: Gothic tribes, under leaders such as Cniva, conducted major invasions across the Danube frontier into Roman Moesia and Thrace. These invasions were part of sustained pressure on the Roman frontier during the Third Century Crisis. The Goths were seeking plunder and possibly migration routes into the empire. Trebonianus Gallus faced significant military pressure from these invasions.

sources.name: Historia Augusta and later Roman sources

events.certainty_note: Gothic invasions during this period are attested in later sources, but precise dating to individual years is difficult. The chronology of Gothic invasions (249–253 CE) is debated.
cross_references: Part of broader Gothic pressure on Roman frontiers (240s–270s CE)
Led to the Battle of Abrittus (251 CE) or subsequent conflicts

disconfirming_evidence: The exact chronology of Gothic invasions in the early 250s CE is disputed. Some sources place major invasions in 249–251 CE, others in 252–253 CE. The Historia Augusta is unreliable for this period. Modern scholarship questions whether individual 'invasions' were discrete events or part of continuous raiding and migration. The Plague of Cyprian's identity and extent remain debated.
historiographic_note: 252 CE is poorly documented. The Historia Augusta is the primary source for Trebonianus Gallus' reign, but it is notoriously unreliable. Later sources like Zosimus provide additional information but are also not contemporary. Archaeological evidence is crucial but unevenly distributed. The narrative is heavily dependent on fragmentary sources and later compilations. Non-Mediterranean regions are almost entirely absent from surviving documentation.

graph_edges.note: Plague of Cyprian continued across multiple years
Gothic invasions were part of sustained frontier pressure
